extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Thermal incubator system comprising an insulated box with a heatbed, sensors, communication software, controller, and simulation models. Operational domain involves temperature regulation in controlled environments. Key characteristics include physical-thermal dynamics, real-time monitoring capabilities, and integration with cyber-physical systems (CPS) for bidirectional data exchange between physical and digital domains.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboard; on-demand calibration using automated parameter optimization; anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation via Kalman filter; what-if simulations through co-simulations with historical/real-time data; self-adaptation loop enabling dynamic parameter adjustment when new objects (e.g., cold water bucket) are introduced. Services target engineers/system operators for monitoring, predictive maintenance, and control optimization.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: RabbitMQ [4] as message broker for synchronization/communication; low-level driver for sensor-actuator interaction with safety protocols; bang-bang controller for temperature regulation; simulation models (Model A/B) with calibrated parameters (C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater); time series database for data logging; Kalman filter algorithm for state estimation; co-simulation framework for what-if scenarios. Technologies include RabbitMQ (message brokering), simulation models (thermal inertia prediction), and state estimation algorithms.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ communication server, with calibration processes executed on-demand and what-if simulations using historical/real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT). Kalman filter for anomaly detection operates in real-time to estimate heatbed states.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple digital twin instances (Model A and Model B) with centralized architecture via RabbitMQ for coordination. The controller and low-level driver operate as distinct components with hierarchical responsibilities: controller manages temperature regulation, low-level driver handles PT state reporting, and communication server orchestrates data flow between instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated architecture integrating RabbitMQ as message broker, Kalman filter for state estimation, and co-simulation frameworks for what-if scenarios. Models (A/B) are calibrated with parameters (C_air, G_box, etc.) and dynamically updated via calibration algorithms. Monitors and optimizers are integrated to check system safety and performance.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems via RabbitMQ message broker for data exchange. Dependencies include open-source tools (RabbitMQ, GitHub repository) and interoperability with simulation models (Model A/B) for co-simulation. No explicit mention of enterprise systems or standards, but data is shared between PT and DT through communication server.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include two simulation models (Model A and Model B) for predicting incubator temperature. Model A has 2 free parameters (C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K), while Model B has 4 parameters (C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K, C_heater=33.65 J/K, G_heater=0.87 J/K). Data is collected from the Physical Twin (PT) via sensors over time, stored in a time series database, and used for real-time visualization, anomaly detection (Kalman filter), and co-simulations. Models are integrated with calibration algorithms to update parameters and inform monitors for safety checks.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The incubator's physical system includes a heatbed (actuator for thermal control) and a controller (software system for communication and simulation models). Actuation ranges are defined by the heatbed's thermal inertia, with safety constraints enforced through monitoring. Remote control interfaces involve the controller and CAN (Controller Area Network) for cyber-physical coordination.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors measure temperature (for heatbed state estimation) and fan voltage (Figure 12). Data collection mechanisms include time series databases, with sampling frequencies and transmission rates implied by calibration experiments (e.g., Figure 13). Accuracy specifications are inferred from model calibration results, though exact precision values are not explicitly stated.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated through calibration experiments (e.g., Model B outperforms Model A in predicting heatbed inertia). Uncertainty sources include sensor inaccuracies and parameter estimation errors. Error correction mechanisms involve Kalman filters for state estimation and iterative calibration. Trade-offs between model accuracy (e.g., high-parameter models) and computational efficiency are noted, with Model B prioritizing thermal inertia prediction.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (e.g., DS18S20 temperature sensors) to digital systems via 1-Wire bus and RabbitMQ. Data types include temperature readings (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy). Communication protocols: 1-Wire, RabbitMQ (MQTT-like). Transmission frequency: periodic (low-level driver reads temperature data). Event triggers: system state changes, calibration events. Data quality assurance: calibration algorithms, fault detectors. Preprocessing: data normalization, filtering. Integration mechanisms: RabbitMQ message queues, time series databases.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan/heatbed control) sent via RabbitMQ to low-level drivers. Command types: actuator control signals. Message formats: JSON-like structures via RabbitMQ. Validation: safe protocol in low-level driver. Feedback: temperature sensor data. Conflict resolution: prioritization of safety-critical commands. Execution confirmation: state updates reported to RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocols: RabbitMQ (MQTT-like), 1-Wire. Network topology: edge (Raspberry Pi) with RabbitMQ server. Bandwidth: unspecified. Latency: low (RabbitMQ's lightweight design). Reliability: safe protocol in low-level driver, redundant message queues. Security: TLS/SSL (implied by RabbitMQ's default security features).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting: edge (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B) with optional cloud integration. Computational: Raspberry Pi's ARM Cortex-A53. Storage: time series databases (e.g., InfluxDB). Scalability: modular component architecture. Deployment models: edge-first with cloud extension. Availability: ensured by redundant messaging and safe protocols.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle includes development stages (model calibration, simulation models with 2/4 free parameters), representation types (dynamic models for thermal inertia prediction), transition management (calibration experiments to refine models), and governance via open-source repositories and version control.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering processes involve model calibration (section 3.3), RabbitMQ for synchronization/communication, and testing via co-simulations. Version control is achieved through open-source repositories. Evolution strategies include updating models with calibrated parameters (e.g., C_air, G_box, C_heater) and integrating real-time data for self-adaptation.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes real-time visualization (dashboard via time series data), anomaly detection (Kalman filter with Model B), what-if simulations (co-simulations for controller optimization), and state estimation (heatbed temperature correlation). Analytics use differential equations and simulation models, with confidence measures derived from Kalman filter accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with open-source standards (RabbitMQ, GitHub repository). Interoperability is achieved via RabbitMQ's message brokering and modular component design (low-level driver, controller, communication server). No specific industry standards or certifications are explicitly mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: A safe protocol is incorporated into the low-level driver to ensure the device remains in a safe state. The low-level driver periodically checks for commands and temperature data, ensuring operational safety. RabbitMQ is used for communication, which supports secure message brokering, though specific cybersecurity protocols are not detailed.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).

employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series

described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].

Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.

historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.

**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and

more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).

employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided

Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,

not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.
